topic: mom wants new izakaya menu design — free work

turns.content: my mom called me today and she wants me to redo the entire menu for her restaurant. like the whole thing, new layout, new photos, everything. and obviously she expects it for free because i'm her son lol

Ha, the classic family expectation — "you're a designer, just whip something up!" As if a full menu redesign with new photography is a weekend project.

To be fair, it's your mom, so there's probably no winning the "let me invoice you" conversation. But you can at least set some boundaries around scope. What's she envisioning — just a visual refresh of the current layout, or a complete overhaul with new categories, descriptions, the works?

complete overhaul. she wants new food photography, a seasonal specials insert, and she keeps saying she wants it to feel "more modern" which like... what does that even mean coming from someone who thinks comic sans is a fun font

"More modern" from a non-designer almost always means "cleaner, more white space, maybe sans-serif." It's one of those wonderfully vague briefs that could mean anything.

The food photography alone is a significant undertaking. Are you planning to shoot it yourself, or would you bring in a food photographer? Because shooting, editing, color correcting, and then laying everything out — that's easily a few full days of work even for family.

Also, the seasonal specials insert is actually a smart idea from a practical standpoint. It means you design the core menu once and just swap out the insert when dishes change. Less rework down the line.

i'll probably shoot it myself honestly. i've been getting more into photography lately and food photography is fun when the food is actually good. and her food is incredible so at least i have that going for me. just wish i was getting paid lol

At least you'll eat well during the shoot — that's a form of payment, right?

If you're shooting it yourself, a few things to keep in mind:

- **Lighting is everything** for food photography. Natural light near a window is ideal, but if you're shooting in the restaurant during service hours, you might need a small softbox or ring light to avoid that harsh overhead fluorescent look.
- **Shoot more than you need** — for a full menu you'll want options for every dish. Better to have 10 shots of each and pick the best one.
- **Warm tones sell food** — lean into warm white balance. Cool-toned food photos make everything look clinical.

For the layout itself, are you thinking bilingual? Japanese and English? That changes the typography considerations a lot.

yeah bilingual for sure, they get some tourists and she wants to be ready for more. the typography is honestly the part i'm most excited about. like the food photos are work but picking the right typeface pairing for a japanese-english menu? that's my happy place

That's the designer brain at work — the part most people wouldn't even notice is the part that excites you the most.

Bilingual menus are a really interesting typographic challenge. You need:

- A Japanese typeface with good readability at small sizes (menu body copy is usually pretty tight)
- A Latin typeface that complements the Japanese without clashing in weight or style
- Consistent visual hierarchy across both languages so neither feels like an afterthought

The tricky part is that Japanese characters are inherently denser than Latin ones, so you have to balance the visual weight. A lot of bilingual menus fail because the English looks too light next to the kanji.

Are you leaning toward something traditional to match the restaurant's vibe, or pushing toward that "modern" direction your mom wants?

i think somewhere in between honestly. like the place has history and character, i don't want to strip that out and make it look like a minimalist cafe menu. but the current design is genuinely dated. i'm thinking maybe a modern serif for english, something with a bit of warmth, paired with a clean gothic for the japanese

That sounds like a really strong direction. A modern serif with warmth — something like Freight Text or Lora — paired with a clean Japanese gothic like Noto Sans JP or Hiragino Kaku Gothic would give you that balance of contemporary and approachable.

The key is making sure the x-height of your Latin typeface sits comfortably alongside the Japanese characters. Test it at actual print size early — what looks great on screen at 200% can feel completely different when you're holding the physical menu.

Also, since the restaurant has history, you could lean into that with the design details — maybe a subtle texture in the background, or a hand-drawn element for section dividers. Modern doesn't have to mean sterile.

What's her timeline on this? Is she expecting it by New Year's?

she said "whenever you have time" which in mom language means she wants it yesterday. i told her january because i actually have paying clients right now and i can't just drop everything for free work, even for her. she guilt-tripped me a little but she gets it

"Whenever you have time" is absolutely mom code for "why isn't it done already." But January is a reasonable timeline, and you're right to prioritize paying work.

Honestly, this could be a great portfolio piece even if it's unpaid. A full menu redesign with custom photography, bilingual typography, and a cohesive brand refresh — that's a solid case study. Especially if you document the before and after.

Maybe frame it that way internally: it's not free work, it's a self-directed project with a real client and real constraints. Takes some of the sting out.

When you start on the typography pairing, it might be worth mocking up two or three options and letting her pick. Gives her ownership of the decision and reduces the "I don't like it, change everything" risk later.

that's actually really good advice, the mockup options thing. last time i did something for her i just presented one version and she had me change it like eight times. should have learned from my actual client workflow lol. okay i feel better about this now. still wish she'd pay me though, even like a symbolic amount. or at least unlimited yakitori

Unlimited yakitori is honestly a better deal than what some clients pay. Negotiate hard on that one.

And yeah — treat her like a real client in terms of process. Mood board, two to three mockup directions, one round of revisions, final delivery. If you set that structure up front, it'll save you from the endless revision spiral.

Good luck with it. The typography pairing is going to be the fun part, and the photography will be delicious at minimum. January is plenty of time if you block out a weekend for the shoot and a few evenings for layout. You've got this.

oh wait i also forgot to mention — she wants me to design the seasonal specials insert so she can swap it out herself. like she wants to be able to change the text in word or something. how do i handle that without the typography falling apart

Oh, the classic "I want to edit it myself" request. This is where most restaurant menus go to die typographically.

The best approach is to set up a template she literally cannot mess up:

1. **Use Google Docs or Canva** for the insert — not Word. Canva especially lets you lock down the layout, fonts, and sizing so she can only edit the text fields you designate. She changes the dish name and price, but the font, spacing, and alignment stay fixed.

2. **Pre-install the fonts** if you go the Docs route, and give her a cheat sheet: "type the dish name here, type the price here, do not change the font size." Keep it as foolproof as possible.

3. **Design the insert with generous margins** so that even if a dish name runs long, the layout doesn't break.

Basically, give her the illusion of control while constraining everything that matters. It's like designing a CMS template — the user gets to change content, not design.

She'll probably still find a way to add clip art, but at least you tried.
